I'm in the same boat, so I understand the pain.

We all use Thunderbird. But many users have needs that vary, and so we try to offer different capabilities.

In version 150, which comes out in a about 7 weeks (sooner in beta), the possibility of alpha order will return - you will be able to change the order to alphabetic, and also be able to set the number of folders shown.

content:
I don't recall a quota bar, but you can check the percentage quota used by right-clicking on a folder and choosing Properties. There's a Quota tab there. Not very convenient, but it's there.

I did find mention of a Quota Bar add-on from long ago that appears to have been abandoned by the author.
creator: rednoise

content: Lin, Thanks for that, very handy. I had no idea it was there, Alan.
creator: Alan37

content: By default your used quota will appear in the status bar when you've reached 75%. You can change that setting in about:config (Config Editor... at the bottom of Settings > General) Look for mail.quota.mainwindow_threshold, there are three tresholds you can set. Set mail.quota.mainwindow_threshold.show to 0 to make your quota always visible.
creator: dillenger

content: dillenger, Thanks, that explains everything, Alan.

content:
POP3, by design, downloads all messages to your local computer and removes them from the server. There are ways to modify that default behavior, but unless you have taken those steps, everything was in your Thunderbird profile and are not available to be downloaded again. You can confirm this by going to your email providers web mail interface.

If you made a backup of your then-current profile, you can restore it and you'll have all your missing emails back, minus any received since then. Before you do that, you should make a backup of the newest profile - the newer messages can be retrieved from there. The problem of the mis-tagged messages can be fixed.

If you didn't back up before doing the restore, your old email is probably gone for good.
creator: rednoise

content:
I do have my Gmail set up to keep the copy in the inbox after accessing via POP. I was in fact still able to see them on the Gmail web interface.

I have multiple email accounts, a couple Gmail and a couple others. Some are POP some are IMAP. I use Thunderbird on the PC and I also have an iPhone. I tend to think of my PC as the "golden copy", meaning I have emails from many years ago, and I often move emails from an inbox to one of several local folders for organization, although the large majority I leave in the original inbox or delete. I do not mind that I do not have all these local folders and subfolders, etc, on my iPhone. One thing I DO NOT want is if I go on the Gmail web interface and delete old emails to free up space, is for them to get deleted from my PC.

Beside today's problem with not re-downloading emails, if you have any tips/advice on configuration I would appreciate it.

Thanks
creator: IamTom

content:
Benno Zimmermann said
mail.ru (the most used Russian mail provider) and vk.com (a russian messenger app) recently decided to cooperate closely. On Linux desktop trying to use webmail (using firefox) to reach mail.ru, I have to solve a captcha (put to me by vk.com) everytime (extremely difficult, since there is a slider to move until you see distinct Russian words.Btw. I know Russian, but I struggle). Installing it in thunderbird using imap.mail.ru and smtp. mail.ru and setting the TLS and port numbers, this is what all sites tell you to do when you search, absolutely doesn't work, even if you set your vk identity (which I know) as a password instead of the mail password or the vk.com password. Before this mail.ru/vk.com cooperation, I could easily use mail.ru just via imap etc., using my normal password. This is definitely gone. On android however, it's easy: There is a mail.ru app in the playstore, working very well. I don't want to use mail.ru on android, since typing on android is cumbersome.
creator: benno.m.zimmermann
